pmos

Product Sense and Taste — Primer

Created using pmos‑learnkit

Primer · PM Tactics — Skills & Processes. Product sense is not a sixth sense. The practitioner canon converges on a definition and a posture: Jules Walter calls it "the skill of consistently being able to craft products… that have the intended impact on their users" and insists "it's a learned skill, just like any other PM skill"; Nikkel Blaase adds the business dimension and the four ingredients (empathy, domain knowledge, creative problem-solving, experience). Julie Zhuo grounds it in two habits — observation and inquiry — and is blunt that "growth comes from practice." The mechanism for building it is deconstruction: structured product critique ("there is no shortcut… besides keen and close observation"), repeated until it becomes a pattern library, plus Walter's product-sense loop of assumption → experiment → reflection. Its sibling, taste, is "an instinct for what feels right," and Marty Cagan keeps intuition honest ("most of your ideas suck" → test). It's graded explicitly in interviews. The live debate: whether the creative core is fully learnable or partly innate. Provenance: practitioner-canon synthesis. 15 verified sources.

What product sense actually is

The most useful thing about the modern product-sense canon is that its leading voices broadly agree on a definition, which lets us treat "product sense" as a specific, gradeable skill rather than a vibe. Jules Walter's formulation, published in Lenny Rachitsky's newsletter and widely treated as the reference definition, is "the skill of consistently being able to craft products (or make changes to existing products) that have the intended impact on their users." The operative word is consistently — anyone can be right once; product sense is being right about which changes will land, repeatedly and across contexts.

Nikkel Blaase extends the definition to include the commercial side, which matters for senior PMs whose calls are judged on business outcomes as well as user love: "Good Product Sense is the acquired ability to design products or changes to existing products in a way that has the intended effect on their users or customers and also has a positive effect on the business itself." Note the word "acquired" — it is doing the same work as Walter's "learned," pre-empting the innate-gift framing before the definition is even finished.

Academy of PM sharpens one more edge that separates product sense from personal preference: it is "looking beyond your opinion and preference about a product to anticipate how the broader market will react." This is the discipline that makes product sense hard for smart people — it requires holding your own reaction at arm's length and predicting a population's, which is a fundamentally empathetic and empirical act rather than an aesthetic one. The rest of this primer is about where that prediction skill comes from and how to build it deliberately.

Further reading: blog.hubspot.com — Explains enterprise sales and how it differs from SMB and mid-market sales across eight dimensions including deal length, number of decision makers, and purchase complexity, with guidance on building an enterprise sales strategy.

Sense, taste, and judgment — distinguishing the terms

These three words get used interchangeably, but the canon treats them as related-but-distinct, and the distinctions are worth holding because they point at different practices. Product sense, per the definitions above, is predictive: will this change have the intended impact on users and the business? It is the most empirical of the three — it can be checked against outcomes.

Taste, as the Mind the Product essay frames it, is narrower and more aesthetic-adjacent: "the ability to see beyond what is simply functional or trendy. It's about having an instinct for what feels right." Where sense asks "will this work," taste asks "is this excellent" — it operates on the craft dimension, the difference between a product that functions and one people love. The essay is explicit that taste shows up in details: "that product was built by a team that cared deeply about the details." Sense without taste ships things that work but feel mediocre; taste without sense ships beautiful things nobody needs.

Judgment is the broadest of the three — the meta-skill of knowing which of your instincts to trust in a given situation, and when to override them with data. Much of the canon on product sense traces to Julie Zhuo, whose authority here is grounded: per her biography she "was the vice president of product design at Facebook," having "joined Facebook in May 2006 as its first intern," and wrote "The Making of a Manager." Her framing of the underlying skill as "product thinking" — "The skill of knowing what makes a product useful — and loved — by people" — usefully spans all three: useful is sense, loved is taste, and knowing which lever a given product needs is judgment.

Innate or learned?

This is the load-bearing debate of the entire topic, and the practitioner consensus comes down hard on one side — with one honest hedge. The dominant camp says product sense is built, not born. Walter: "product sense is not something you need to be born with. It's a learned skill, just like any other PM skill." Blaase: "this is not an innate ability, but something that can be learned and developed over time." Zhuo, on the sibling skill: "As with all skills, it can be nurtured and developed; it's not just an instinct one does or doesn't have," and "Excelling at product thinking is not innate; growth comes from practice." Academy of PM is the bluntest: "Product sense is not magic. It's not a sixth sense that comes out of thin air. It's a skill that you can learn," likening it to cooking.

Why the unanimity matters: if you believe sense is innate, you stop practicing and you mis-hire, screening for confident taste instead of trainable curiosity. The "learned" framing is not just encouraging — it changes what you do tomorrow. Zhuo's analogy is the most pointed rebuttal of the gift theory: product design "isn't some innate skill one is born with, like having a good ear for distinguishing notes or possessing exceptionally powerful twitch muscles for sprinting across an Olympic stage."

The honest hedge, worth flagging because it comes from a pro-learnable source rather than a skeptic: Yale's career guide concedes the creative ingredient may be partly innate — "You can't teach creativity; it's one of those things that you're mostly born with. It will be challenging if you aren't naturally gifted here, but it isn't impossible." The reconciliation most of the canon would accept: the empathy, observation, and pattern-recognition components are squarely learnable; the creative-leap component has a talent floor that practice raises but may not fully erase. This is a genuine, unresolved nuance rather than a settled fact — see the contested section for the dissenting "innate" framing that survives in the interview-prep literature.

The ingredients of product sense

If sense is learnable, the natural next question is "made of what?" — and here the canon is remarkably consistent. Blaase's four-factor decomposition is the one most others echo verbatim: "empathy to discover meaningful user needs, product and domain knowledge of how successful solutions in the market work, creative problem-solving to effectively meet user and business needs alike, and finally, experience gained over time in product development." Walter compresses the same set to two leading ingredients — empathy "to discover meaningful user needs" and creativity "to come up with solutions that effectively address those needs" — with domain knowledge and experience as the substrate both draw on.

The decomposition is not academic; it is a diagnostic. A PM who ships things that work but feel obvious is usually long on domain knowledge and short on creative problem-solving. A PM with dazzling ideas that miss is often long on creativity and short on empathy. Yale's version names the same trio — sense "combines empathy, domain knowledge, and creativity" — and prescribes treatments per ingredient (talk to users for empathy; study cognitive biases and behavioral economics to sharpen prediction; deconstruct your domain's apps for product knowledge). Knowing which ingredient is your bottleneck is the first move in building sense deliberately.

Empathy: the foundation ingredient

Across every source, empathy is the load-bearing ingredient — the one without which the others cannot fire, because sense is fundamentally a prediction about other people's reactions, not your own. Zhuo decomposes the empathy skill into two tenets that are more concrete than the word usually implies: developing good product intuition "is about two core tenets: understanding people's desires, and understanding how people react to things." The first is about latent needs; the second is about observed behavior — and the gap between what people say they want and how they actually react is where most product mistakes live.

The practice Zhuo prescribes is observation followed by inquiry: observation is "paying attention to people's reactions when they encounter products or services in their day-to-day lives," and inquiry is "asking the 'why' behind your observations." Walter operationalizes the same instinct as a calendar commitment — attend user research "2-4 times monthly," watching "micro-expressions" and asking "open-ended questions." The senior-PM trap here is substituting your own fluency for the user's: the more expert you become in your domain, the further your reactions drift from a normal user's, which is exactly why the empathy reps have to stay continuous rather than tapering as you gain seniority.

Building a pattern library

If empathy supplies the raw signal, a pattern library is how that signal compounds into fast, accurate intuition. The mechanism the canon keeps returning to is deconstruction: take products apart, ask why each choice was made, and accumulate a mental catalog of what-works-when that you can pattern-match against in the moment. Walter's version is to "deconstruct products" for "1-2 hours monthly," "comparing competing offerings, and analyzing your own reactions."

Sam Polgar makes the deconstruction repeatable with a before/during/after loop: before use, "identify marketed value, desired outcomes, and reference sources"; during, "assess onboarding, intuitiveness, features, comparisons, engagement, thoughts, and feelings"; after, "evaluate outcome achievement, alignment between marketing and experience, doubts, and improvement opportunities." The point of asking the same questions across many products is that "by consistently asking the same questions about different products, practitioners develop pattern recognition" — the library only becomes searchable when its entries share a schema. Polgar adds an underrated caveat senior PMs ignore at their peril: "your mental and emotional state will affect your Product Sense," so the reps only deposit useful patterns when done with "an inquisitive and empathetic mind" and the "ability to listen without bias or prejudice," not while half-distracted in a backlog grooming session.

The product critique

The product critique is the canonical, structured form of deconstruction — the single most-recommended exercise for building sense, popularized by Zhuo's "How to Do a Product Critique." She frames it as curiosity made systematic: "exploring the curiosity of why some products and experiences work for people, and why others don't," anchored by the claim that "there is no shortcut to developing better product instincts besides keen and close observation."

Her structure splits the experience across time, because different truths surface at different moments. Before opening an app: "How did this app come to your attention?" and "What's your one-line summary of what this app does?" In the first minutes: "What's the experience of getting started or signing up?", "How does this app explain itself in the first minute?", and crucially "How did you feel while exploring the app?" — feeling, not just function. The timing matters because "Most opinions of a product are formed within the first few minutes," so a critique that skips the first-run experience misses where the product is actually won or lost.

Rohan Sandeep distills the same three-stage frame — "before, first time usage, and weeks after" — into four interface questions a user should be able to answer "without the baggage of technical and interface flow knowledge": "Where am I, Why Am I here, What can I do, and how can I do it." He recommends comparative critique to sharpen the eye ("How does Snapchat compare with Instagram, or how does chrome compare with Safari") and ties the practice to the classic cognitive-walkthrough method. The senior-PM upgrade is to critique adversarially: not "do I like this" but "why did a competent team make this exact choice, and what would have to be true for it to be right?"

Further reading: blog.hubspot.com — Explains enterprise sales and how it differs from SMB and mid-market sales across eight dimensions including deal length, number of decision makers, and purchase complexity, with guidance on building an enterprise sales strategy.

The product-sense loop

Critique builds sense by observing others' finished products; the product-sense loop builds it by closing the feedback cycle on your own calls. This is the practice that converts experience into sense rather than just accumulating tenure — the mechanism by which years on the job actually compound. Walter's loop is to identify your product and user assumptions, construct experiments to prove or disprove them, and then "deeply analyze outcomes and reflect on why you were right or wrong."

The reflection step is the one most PMs skip, and skipping it is why some PMs have ten years of experience and others have one year ten times. A shipped feature that succeeds teaches nothing if you never articulated what you predicted and why; you cannot tell luck from sense without a recorded prior. Academy of PM names the same engine more generally as "Observation, Curiosity, [and] Repetition," with the unglamorous punchline "Getting great at anything takes practice. Do the work." The loop is what makes the practice deliberate: a guess, a stated reason, an outcome, and an honest post-mortem on the gap. Run enough times, the gap narrows — that narrowing is product sense improving.

Data and intuition

Senior PMs live in the tension between gut and dashboard, and the canon's position is not "trust your sense" but "discipline your sense with evidence." Marty Cagan is the sobering voice here. His humility check on raw intuition is the line "Most of your ideas suck," with the corollary that teams should "kill at least half their ideas" — and the resolution is testing over arguing: "In a good product culture, you don't have endless meetings, you don't have a bunch of escalations; you test." Intuition's job, in this view, is to generate good hypotheses fast; data's job is to adjudicate them. Sense that refuses to be tested is just opinion with seniority.

But the canon equally warns against the opposite failure — letting data smother judgment. The Mind the Product taste essay frames the synthesis as combining "insights from data and user feedback with a vision that is aesthetically pleasing, intuitive, and deeply aligned with users' needs." Data tells you what happened; it cannot tell you what to build next, because the most important products often have no precedent to measure. Cagan's posture — "be stubborn on vision, but flexible on details" — is the practical settlement: hold the intuition-driven vision firm enough to survive a bad quarter, while letting data freely overrule your guesses about the details. The skill is knowing which calls are vision (defend) and which are details (test and update).

Further reading: uxdesign.cc — Analysis of Google's "messy middle" consumer decision-making model for product managers, explaining how customers oscillate between exploration and evaluation before purchase and how products can influence that journey.

Taste and craft

Taste is where product sense meets quality — the instinct that separates products that merely work from products people love, and it lives almost entirely in details. Mind the Product's definition is "an instinct for what feels right," and its diagnostic is observational: "Think of any digital product that you love… that product was built by a team that cared deeply about the details." Craft, in this framing, is not polish applied at the end; it is the accumulated evidence that someone with taste made a thousand small calls correctly.

The harder question for a senior PM is whether taste is buildable, and here the taste essay is more pessimistic than the product-sense canon — it leans organizational rather than individual: "great design is not just a discipline or a process—it's an intuition, an inherent understanding of what will resonate with users," and the prescribed lever is "hiring and nurturing people with a strong sense of product design intuition" plus "an environment where this kind of thinking is valued." It offers a concrete ritual — a "Why can't we have nice things?" workshop where teams study exceptional designs to "raise the bar" and build "a shared vision of excellence" — which suggests taste is at least transmissible within a team even if it is slower to install in an individual than product sense. Worth flagging: this organizational, harder-to-teach view of taste sits in mild tension with the strongly-learnable view of product sense, and the canon does not fully reconcile them.

Further reading: hbr.org — Harvard Business Review analysis of Apple's organizational structure: a functional model where experts lead experts, enabling innovation at scale, as Apple grew from $7B to $260B in revenue under this leadership model.

Product sense under evaluation

One of the clearest windows onto what "good product sense" concretely means is how companies test for it, because the interview forces the abstract skill into observable signals. Exponent's guide names five: user empathy ("Do you genuinely think from the user's perspective, or do you jump straight to solutions?"), structured thinking ("Can you break an ambiguous problem into a clear, navigable process?"), product taste ("Are your ideas interesting, specific, and grounded in real user value, or generic?"), strategic awareness, and communication. Notably, the bar has moved: "a structured answer used to be a reasonable signal. Now it is a baseline" — which means a memorized framework no longer demonstrates sense; it just gets you to the starting line.

This is the consensus across the interview-prep canon: process over answer. ProdPad — "The whole point of this interview is to see your reasoning, so make sure you're prepared to go over every step of your decision-making process" — and the recurring observation that interviewers "aren't too interested in your final answer" but in "how your product brain works." Product School catalogs the frameworks candidates lean on (CIRCLES, STAR, BUS) while conceding what they actually screen for is "a candidate's ability to empathize with users and to innovate within market constraints." The senior-PM takeaway extends beyond hiring: the same five signals are how your sense is judged in any product review, so making your reasoning legible — narrating the user, the problem, the options, the call — is itself part of demonstrating sense, not separate from it.

Further reading: propel.app — Propel describes building an automated LLM evaluation suite for SNAP (food stamp) program questions, covering how to define accuracy, automate test cases, and use evals to compare models and identify safety-critical edge cases.

Building it deliberately

Pulling the practices together, the canon describes a coherent regimen rather than a grab-bag, and its through-line is deliberate repetition with feedback. Zhuo's cadence is the simplest entry point: "Every week, try at least one new product, feature, or service" and "have at least one conversation or reflection about how a specific product decision impacts its intended audience." That weekly rhythm seeds both the pattern library (new products) and the loop (reflection on decisions).

Layer the ingredient-specific treatments on top. For empathy, Yale prescribes "talk to different types of users and partners" and studying "cognitive biases, including behavioral economics" — because predicting reactions is easier when you know the systematic ways human judgment bends. For domain knowledge, deconstruct your domain's apps in detail: "why this button was put in this place, or why this animation was done in that manner." For the compounding engine, Academy of PM's "Observation, Curiosity, Repetition" with its five-question drill (what's great, what's not, the company's goal and target market, did they nail it, how would I make it better) gives a portable critique you can run on anything in five minutes. The meta-move for a senior PM is to make these reps non-negotiable calendar commitments rather than aspirations — sense decays without practice, and seniority, by pulling you away from users, accelerates the decay if you let it.

What's contested and evolving

The strong consensus that product sense is learnable has a persistent dissenting strand, and it is worth naming because it survives in influential places. The interview-prep literature in particular still reaches for innate language: Product School defines product sense as "the innate ability to understand what makes a product great and how it can meet the market's needs" — directly contradicting Walter, Blaase, Zhuo, and Academy of PM. The most defensible reconciliation, supported by Yale's hedge that creativity is "mostly born with," is that the empathy and pattern-recognition components are squarely trainable while the creative-leap component has a talent floor — but this is an unresolved nuance, and you will encounter confident claims on both sides.

A second live debate is framework-versus-mindset. The PM-interview-prep camp argues frameworks have become a crutch: "Landing a product manager role requires more than just reciting frameworks," and tools "like the CIRCLES Method or the AARRR Framework… should be seen as starting points rather than rigid blueprints" because "Real-world product challenges rarely fit neatly into predefined boxes." Exponent's "structured answer… is now a baseline" says the same thing from the evaluator's side. The disagreement isn't whether frameworks help — it's whether teaching them builds sense or substitutes for it; the honest answer is that a framework is scaffolding you should outgrow.

Finally, the topic is being reshaped in real time by AI. As of 2026, the balance between human taste and AI-assisted generation, and what "product sense" means when an AI can produce a dozen plausible designs on request, is actively unsettled — treat any fixed claim about the future of product judgment as a snapshot of a moving target rather than a settled answer.

Where this connects — adjacent topics

  • Product discovery & user research — the empathy engine that feeds product sense its raw signal; continuous discovery is how senior PMs keep their intuition calibrated to real users.
  • Product judgment & decision-making — the broader meta-skill of knowing which instincts to trust and when to override them with data.
  • Design & UX fundamentals — where taste and craft live; the vocabulary of details that a product critique trains you to see.
  • Product strategy & vision — the "stubborn on vision" half of the data-vs-intuition settlement; sense at the level of which bets to make, not just which features.
  • Metrics & experimentation — the adjudication machinery the product-sense loop runs its assumptions through; how you tell luck from sense.
  • PM interviews & hiring — where product sense is made observable and gradeable; the five signals double as a self-assessment rubric.

References

  1. blog.hubspot.com ·cited· (T3)

    Explains enterprise sales and how it differs from SMB and mid-market sales across eight dimensions including deal length, number of decision makers, and purchase complexity, with guidance on building an enterprise sales strategy.

  2. blog.hubspot.com ·cited· (T3)

    Explains enterprise sales and how it differs from SMB and mid-market sales across eight dimensions including deal length, number of decision makers, and purchase complexity, with guidance on building an enterprise sales strategy.

  3. hbr.org ·cited· (T3)

    Harvard Business Review analysis of Apple's organizational structure: a functional model where experts lead experts, enabling innovation at scale, as Apple grew from $7B to $260B in revenue under this leadership model.

  4. uxdesign.cc ·cited· (T3)

    Analysis of Google's "messy middle" consumer decision-making model for product managers, explaining how customers oscillate between exploration and evaluation before purchase and how products can influence that journey.

  5. propel.app ·cited· (T3)

    Propel describes building an automated LLM evaluation suite for SNAP (food stamp) program questions, covering how to define accuracy, automate test cases, and use evals to compare models and identify safety-critical edge cases.

  6. lennysnewsletter.com ·cited· (primary-practitioner)

    Jules Walter's guest essay is the canonical definition: product sense is "the skill of consistently being able to craft products (or make changes to existing products) that have the intended impact on their users," and — the load-bearing claim for the whole topic — "product sense is not something you need to be born with. It's a learned skill, just like any other PM skill." He grounds it in two ingredients, empathy "to discover meaningful user needs" and creativity "to come up with solutions that effectively address those needs," and prescribes concrete reps: observe users in research 2–4 times monthly, and deconstruct products 1–2 hours monthly by "examining new applications, comparing competing offerings, and analyzing your own reactions." The progress signal is that you start to "notice subtle things about products and people that you would have missed before."

  7. medium.com ·cited· (primary-practitioner)

    Nikkel Blaase widens Walter's definition to include the business: "Good Product Sense is the acquired ability to design products or changes to existing products in a way that has the intended effect on their users or customers and also has a positive effect on the business itself." He gives the four-factor decomposition most other writers echo — "empathy to discover meaningful user needs, product and domain knowledge of how successful solutions in the market work, creative problem-solving to effectively meet user and business needs alike, and finally, experience gained over time in product development" — and is explicit on the central debate: "this is not an innate ability, but something that can be learned and developed over time."

  8. future.com ·cited· (primary-practitioner)

    Julie Zhuo's a16z/Future essay frames the adjacent skill, product thinking — "The skill of knowing what makes a product useful — and loved — by people" — and names its two habits: observation, "paying attention to people's reactions when they encounter products or services in their day-to-day lives," and inquiry, "asking the 'why' behind your observations." Her stance on the innate-vs-learned question is unambiguous: "As with all skills, it can be nurtured and developed; it's not just an instinct one does or doesn't have," and "Excelling at product thinking is not innate; growth comes from practice." Her prescribed cadence is concrete: "Every week, try at least one new product, feature, or service" and "have at least one conversation or reflection about how a specific product decision impacts its intended audience."

  9. medium.com ·cited· (primary-practitioner)

    Zhuo's product-critique piece operationalizes how to build sense by close observation: a critique is "exploring the curiosity of why some products and experiences work for people, and why others don't," and she insists "Product design isn't some innate skill one is born with, like having a good ear for distinguishing notes or possessing exceptionally powerful twitch muscles." Her structured questions — "What's the experience of getting started or signing up?", "How does this app explain itself in the first minute?", "How did you feel while exploring the app?" — turn a vague gut reaction into examinable evidence, anchored by the observation that "Most opinions of a product are formed within the first few minutes." The closing discipline: "There is no shortcut to developing better product instincts besides keen and close observation."

  10. rohansandeep.medium.com ·cited· (secondary-curation)

    Rohan Sandeep gives a reusable critique scaffold built on Zhuo's three-stage frame — "before, first time usage, and weeks after" — and four interface questions a user should be able to answer without technical baggage: "Where am I, Why Am I here, What can I do, and how can I do it." He links the practice to building general judgment: examining "Doors, … switches, … Instagram, … ERP applications, … browsers" develops "a larger sense of what a product does, and how it does it," and he recommends comparative deconstruction ("How does Snapchat compare with Instagram, or how does chrome compare with Safari") plus the classic cognitive-walkthrough method. Critique is the lab where pattern-recognition gets reps.

  11. sampolgar.medium.com ·cited· (secondary-curation)

    Sam Polgar's synthesis lands the consensus line — "Product Sense is a learned ability — not an unattainable, god-given gift" — and supplies a repeatable before/during/after deconstruction loop: before use, "identify marketed value, desired outcomes, and reference sources"; during, "assess onboarding, intuitiveness, features, comparisons, engagement, thoughts, and feelings"; after, "evaluate outcome achievement, alignment between marketing and experience, doubts, and improvement opportunities." By "consistently asking the same questions about different products, practitioners develop pattern recognition." He adds an underrated caveat — "your mental and emotional state will affect your Product Sense" — so the reps only pay off when done with "an inquisitive and empathetic mind" and the "ability to listen without bias or prejudice."

  12. blog.academyofpm.com ·cited· (secondary-curation)

    Academy of PM frames sense as critical-plus-market thinking — "Product sense is thinking creatively and critically about a product's strengths and weaknesses" and "looking beyond your opinion and preference about a product to anticipate how the broader market will react" — and rejects mystique outright: "Product sense is not magic. It's not a sixth sense that comes out of thin air. It's a skill that you can learn," likening it to cooking. Its three development methods are "Observation, Curiosity, [and] Repetition" ("Getting great at anything takes practice. Do the work"), with a five-question drill: what's great, what's not, what's the company's goal and target market, did they nail it, and how would I make it better.

  13. mindtheproduct.com ·cited· (secondary-curation)

    This Mind the Product essay defines the sibling concept, taste, as "the ability to see beyond what is simply functional or trendy. It's about having an instinct for what feels right," combining "insights from data and user feedback with a vision that is aesthetically pleasing, intuitive, and deeply aligned with users' needs." It is unapologetic that craft is the tell — "that product was built by a team that cared deeply about the details" — and that "great design is not just a discipline or a process—it's an intuition." On whether taste can be grown, it leans organizational: "hiring and nurturing people with a strong sense of product design intuition" and creating "an environment where this kind of thinking is valued," via rituals like a "Why can't we have nice things?" workshop to "raise the bar."

  14. mindtheproduct.com ·cited· (primary-practitioner)

    Marty Cagan supplies the craft-and-judgment anchor: "Our job as product managers is to be stubborn on vision, but flexible on details" — the posture that lets taste operate without becoming dogma. His humility check on raw intuition is the line "Most of your ideas suck," with the corollary that teams should "kill at least half their ideas" and "test" rather than argue ("In a good product culture, you don't have endless meetings, you don't have a bunch of escalations; you test"). And he frames where instinct comes from organizationally — "If you're just using your developers to code, you're only getting half their value" — locating product judgment in a team that confronts real customer problems, not in a lone visionary.

  15. tryexponent.com ·cited· (secondary-curation)

    Exponent's guide reveals what "good product sense" looks like when it's being graded, naming five signals interviewers score: user empathy ("Do you genuinely think from the user's perspective, or do you jump straight to solutions?"), structured thinking ("Can you break an ambiguous problem into a clear, navigable process?"), product taste ("Are your ideas interesting, specific, and grounded in real user value, or generic?"), strategic awareness, and communication. The bar has risen: "a structured answer used to be a reasonable signal. Now it is a baseline," so execution quality, not a memorized framework, separates candidates. One staff PM's priority order was breaking ambiguity into structure fast, organized communication, and digging "into personas with real detail."

  16. productschool.com ·cited· (secondary-curation)

    Product School is the useful counter-voice on the innate question — it calls product sense "the innate ability to understand what makes a product great and how it can meet the market's needs," language most practitioner-canon explicitly rejects, which is why the debate is live rather than settled. Its practical contribution is the framework menu interviewers see candidates lean on: CIRCLES ("Comprehend, Identify, Report, Customers, List solutions, Evaluate trade-offs, Summarize"), STAR, and BUS ("Business objectives, User problems, Solutions"). What it says interviewers actually want is constant across sources: "a candidate's ability to empathize with users and to innovate within market constraints."

  17. prodpad.com ·cited· (secondary-curation)

    ProdPad reframes the interview as a window onto a career-long skill: "Product sense isn't something that you should focus on just to master the product sense interview. No, this is a skill that you'll want to develop your entire career." On evaluation it stresses reasoning over answers — "The whole point of this interview is to see your reasoning, so make sure you're prepared to go over every step of your decision-making process" — and enumerates what good looks like: "thinking about and being empathetic to users, … strong problem definition, … intuitive design standards, and that you can think strategically." The best evidence is lived: "tie their answers back to real-world experiences, showcasing how their product sense has led them to make effective decisions in past roles."

  18. cdo.som.yale.edu ·cited· (secondary-curation)

    Yale's career-development guide treats sense as buildable — "the ability to design products that address user needs" that "combines empathy, domain knowledge, and creativity" — with prescriptions to "talk to different types of users and partners," study "cognitive biases, including behavioral economics," and deconstruct your domain's apps ("why this button was put in this place, or why this animation was done in that manner"). It is also a candid dissenting note on one ingredient: "You can't teach creativity; it's one of those things that you're mostly born with. It will be challenging if you aren't naturally gifted here, but it isn't impossible" — a useful hedge that even pro-learnable sources concede creativity may be the partially-innate component.

  19. pminterviewprep.club ·cited· (secondary-curation)

    This piece carries the framework-skeptic camp: "Landing a product manager role requires more than just reciting frameworks," and tools "like the CIRCLES Method or the AARRR Framework are valuable tools, but they should be seen as starting points rather than rigid blueprints" because "Real-world product challenges rarely fit neatly into predefined boxes." Real product sense is "the ability to understand users, identify opportunities, and envision solutions that create value," cultivated through user empathy, analytical skill, and strategic thinking — not memorization. Its honest closing: "Cultivating a strong product sense takes time and dedicated effort. It's a journey of continuous learning and improvement."

  20. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (secondary-curation)

    Wikipedia anchors the provenance of the most-cited product-sense voice: Julie Zhuo "was the vice president of product design at Facebook and now co-founder at Sundial," having "joined Facebook in May 2006 as its first intern and became a manager at age 25," and authored "The Making of a Manager: What to Do When Everyone Looks to You" (2019). The biographical grounding matters because so much product-sense canon traces to her writing on design intuition — citing the practitioner's actual track record is part of the trust discipline.

pmos Source: docs/pmos/primer/2026-06-15_product-sense-and-taste.html · Created using pmos‑learnkit
